channel.title: Comments for page "Object Classes"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Object Classes"

item.title: Re: I gotta' question
item.content:encoded: There are a number of esoteric classes this could apply to, namely Apollyon, Cernnunos, Enochian, Ticonderoga, and Uncontainable. Unless there's some conceptual/semantic nonsense going on, I find it hard to believe that the Foundation would ever say that an anomaly is categorically uncontainable, though.

item.title: Re: I gotta' question
item.content:encoded:
is there an object class for something that is impossible to contain?

Most objects like this are Keter. A few use "Uncontained" or some other more esoteric class.

also, if there isn't one, then can someone tell me what I can do to make it one?

You can write whatever you want after ”Object Class:" if no more than 10 people hate it than like it, it's a thing.

item.title: I gotta' question
item.content:encoded:
is there an object class for something that is impossible to contain?

also, if there isn't one, then can someone tell me what I can do to make it one?

I'm not just saying that so I could use it for my own personal use, because I really think that it would add to the creativity of the SCP foundation articles if there was an object class for something that is impossible to contain

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it possible the page will be updated to provide a link to the (somewhat new) Anomaly Classification System that has been developed, or is it not officially recognised? I'm not saying we should put its information here, just have a link to it as an alternative classification system writers can use, and also so it's easier to navigate to.

item.title: Re: 2014 Guide Overhaul
item.content:encoded:
I would like to suggest a revision to the object class system that is split into 3 values

Are there any pages on the mainsite that use your system? What you're proposing seems way more complicated than necessary, and if authors don't see it used successfully first, it's going to be very difficult to convince anyone it's worth implementing.

item.title: Re: 2014 Guide Overhaul
item.content:encoded:
This guide is meant to be reflective of how object classes are understood on the site. If you have a way of doing things you think is better than the current system, then use that system in an article and see if it catches on.

However, given that ACS (which was also a massively-successful optional renovation of the Object Class system) shows no signs of being added to this page (and staff are pretty hesitant to switch up the SEKTEN system for obvious technical reasons), your system would have to be near-totally accepted to be listed in this guide.

It seems like something that would be more appropriate in the Esoteric Classes Guide, under Subclasses, when a successful mainsite article uses it.

item.title: Re: 2014 Guide Overhaul
item.content:encoded: I would like to suggest a revision to the object class system that is split into 3 values C-1- as an example lets break it down C is Containment 1 is equivalent to safe. the first value is always the same the second is a number between 0 and 5 with zero being for Thaumiel, Explained and Neutralized being T E and N respectively. 1-3 being safe through to keter, esoteric classes except apollyon are given 4 and apollyon being given 5. the letter after being a designator for what esoteric class it is for class 4 or weather it is Thaumiel, explained or neutralized for class 0

item.title: Apollyon Class SCPs
item.content:encoded:
Well as of writing this, there are a total of 14 Main List Apollyon SCPs (if you count SCP-001 Proposals) Here's a list…(This will be a list of main list Apollyon Class SCPs and if more get added to the Main List, they will be added on this post).

  • SCP-3999 ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-3999]) by LordStonefish
  • S.D. Locke's Proposal ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/shaggydredlocks-proposal]) by Shaggydredlocks
  • Meta Ike's Proposal ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/jack-ike-s-proposal-i]) by Jack Ike
  • SCP-4023-18 ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-4023]) Scroll down to SCP-4023-18 to see the Object Class "Apollyon" by Utylike
  • SCP-4485 ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-4485/offset/5]) The link that is here is the link to Offset 5. To reach this page from the main list you have to get to Offset 5 (Offsets are each page and clicking "Click to Submit Approval" and if further instructions to reach Offset 5, then look in the Discussion page by Woedenaz and djkaktus
  • SCP-5500 ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/5000contestihp]) By Ihp
  • SCP-4005 ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-4005]) By Tufto
  • SCP-4547 ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-4547]) By stormbreath
  • SCP-4780 ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-4780]) By A Random Day
  • SCP-5055 ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/5000contesttrutherford/offset/1]) Scroll to the bottom of the page to see this by T Rutherford
  • SCP-4057 ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-4057]) by Researcher Blasko
  • SCP-5321 ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/5000contestgrimmcreeper])
+ Spoilers for the Spoiler and 4057
- Close

I know it says Neutralized for The Spoiler and 4057, but it still was and shows that it used to be Apollyon, but it still was Apollyon, so that is why it's here.

  • Jim North's Proposal (SCP-001 Proposal) ([http://scp-wiki.wikidot.com/jim-north-s-proposal/offset/4]) This link will take you to offset 4. By Jim North
  • SCP-5678 ([http://www.scp-wiki.net/scp-5678]) By Oboebandgeek99

item.title: Re: Apollyon
item.content:encoded: Arguably. I'm obviously not the one to ask for a firsthand source, but I believe Keter carried a bit more weight to it when the community was forming; see the inclusion of physical threat in most definitions that feels out of place when Safe and Euclid are entirely concerned with containment difficulty.

item.title: Re: Apollyon
item.content:encoded: Keter "had" a gut reaction?

item.title: Re: Apollyon
item.content:encoded:
You are not the first person to ask this and will probably not be the last. Site consensus at time of writing is that the Apollyon classification is incompatible with the aesthetic and directives of the Foundation. Likewise, codifying the classification would inevitably lead to its abuse in a similar fashion to how Keter has been robbed of any sort of "gut reaction" on the part of the reader.

The tragedy of Apollyon has been well-documented by both Clef and the site at large, but its usage as was originally intended is limited at best. Even as an object class meaning "uncontainable threat", most articles employing Apollyon have either been anomalously contaminated or self-referentially abasing.

Of note is that its initial mystique and intrigue survive in its usage throughout site lore as anything other than an object class — see the House of Apollyon and Tomb of Apollyon referenced throughout djkaktus's articles. Apollyon as an object class is inviable but its potential as a narrative device is exciting as hell.

Also I wrote a character named Apollyon who'll definitely show up again when my Site-17 canon concludes. Eventually. Trust me on that.

item.title: Apollyon
item.content:encoded: Apollyon was originally meant to be a one-time thing, but it seems that it's now a standard object class in all but name (though it's by far the rarest). Should this guide be updated to account for that?

item.title: Another rating thing
item.content:encoded:
If it doesn't fit in the box, it's probably appolyon.
If it doesn't need to go in the box, it's neutralised.

item.title: Re: About the Locked Box Test
item.content:encoded:
Therefore, my suggestion is just the change from the word the to the word a, like this:

That might give the inaccurate impression that all SCPs that are boxes are Thaumiel.

the sentence for the Thaumiel class (without the more detailed explnation on this page) gave me an impression that Thaumiel-classes were some kind of omnipotent entity that could bend reality such that it was uncontainable/contained itself.

I mean… the locked box test isn't meant to be used as a contextless example. I feel like if we were to add some additional detail to the sentence to clarify, it would need to be a different change. Turning "the" to "a" just makes the sentence more vague, since it's supposed to be understood that "the box" refers to the hypothetical box used in the Locked Box test.

item.title: About the Locked Box Test
item.content:encoded:
The Locked Box Test is something that was extremely helpful in my initial week or so in the SCP Foundation community. It helped me understand how the object classes worked and that SCPs weren't classed based on danger, but there's one, tiny suggestion (one word) I want to make about it.

If it is the box, then it's probably Thaumiel.

So the first time I read the Locked Box Test was in the comment section of a video about SCP:CB. All the other classes made perfect sense, but the sentence for the Thaumiel class (without the more detailed explnation on this page) gave me an impression that Thaumiel-classes were some kind of omnipotent entity that could bend reality such that it was uncontainable/contained itself.

Therefore, my suggestion is just the change from the word the to the word a, like this:

If it is a box, then it's probably Thaumiel.

I thoroughly understand the difficulty of changing a page like this one based on a suggestion by a noob like me, and will not be offended if this is rejected :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Button that destroys the entire universe" sounds fun

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How would one know if a button destroys the universe? What, is there a warning printed on the packaging?

item.title: Re: Apologies
item.content:encoded:
More than what we have (which is what? 2?)

I think the Holy City of China 4k entry (whoever's it is) makes 3. Which is still a very small number.

item.title: Re: Apologies
item.content:encoded:
How many main series apollyons do there have to be before it becomes an official object class

More than what we have (which is what? 2?). Beyond that, couldn't say.

Establishing a new Object Class is not like making, say, a new canon or GoI. There's no official number of articles where an object class suddenly becomes official. Rather, it's the result of site culture pushing towards it. And this process shouldn't change, IMO, as altering or adding new Object Classes kinda goes against the at-a-glance function they serve. Make too many of them, make the process too easy, and the system collapses.

item.title: Apologies
item.content:encoded: My apologies, but that still does not answer my original question. How many main series apollyons do there have to be before it becomes an official object class

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Anomalous" can be used as a class to indicate that the object is considered an Anomalous Item, ie, considered too trivial to be catalogued as an SCP. If used on an SCP, it should always have been updated to an actual object class (since it's obviously been upgraded to an SCP).

Example: SCP-472

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anomalous isn't a classification, it simply means that the object breaks the laws of reality in one way or another. All objects contained, regards of classification (minus Explained, obviously) , are anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In a few posts that I've read so far, I've encountered a calssification Anomalous. Is that an actual classification and if so what falls under it? I want to know cause I'm planning on writing an SCP that can't be quite clssified by the means written above.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes, numerous times. Do a search in the forums for something like "unusual object classes" or "non-standard object classes"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Has anyone ever tried making a list of ALL classes? Even the one-offs, and the joke ones.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I've kinda taken over curation for this page, I can say that no, more Apollyon SCPs have not shown up. I rewrote this guide after all of the current Apollyon SCPs were already on the site. And there are currently not enough works that use the class to warrant an explicit mention of it in this guide.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
More apollyon class obejcts have sprung up

…where? My site search for "apollyon" brings up mostly -J articles. Do we have a list somewhere of the main series Apollyons?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: More apollyon class obejcts have sprung up, how many before it becomes an official clasification

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can put that on my "To write" list, but I may not get to it for a few months. That may be something I'd put in my How to Write an SCP rewrite though. In any case, I'll try to work on it at some point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The rewrite looks good. Though, having looked at this page for the first time in potentially years, I can't help but think that there's room for a writing guide about object classes, i.e. how and when they can be used to establish tone or convey the plot. The aim here seems to be making sure readers understand what the classes mean and to help writers pick the object class appropriate for their articles, which is fine, but a bit lacking.

I would write that essay myself, but I don't feel qualified to make any serious judgments or observations on the matter.

item.title: Object Class Guide Rewrite
item.content:encoded:
The Community Outreach team decided that this page needed a rewrite as it did not properly reflect the modern standards and contained information that could mislead new writers. As such, it's been rewritten.

List of changes:

  • Moved Thaumiel and Neutralized from Esoteric Classes to Primary Classes to better reflect their positions in the common canon and to encourage more people to use the classes
  • Removed top-25 list from each Object Class as it didn't provide specific information and could possibly be a small reason new users would copycat them
  • Changed in-universe tone and language to a more causal out of universe tone and language
  • Rewrote object class descriptions to reflect modern standards
  • Removed content that explicitly stated danger was a large part of determining Object Class
  • Rewrote Esoteric Classes to actually reflect their use on the wiki.
  • Shaved a significant amount of fluff or redundant information
  • Removed tab layout so all content appears on a single page
  • Added a question to the FAQ about how danger affects Object Classes.

item.title: Re: Funny thing
item.content:encoded: You do know that this article is not in-universe. This is an out-of-universe guide regarding the Object Class for writers and readers to know about.

item.title: Funny thing
item.content:encoded:
SCP Foundation: "Even the mere existence of Thaumiel-class objects is classified at the highest levels of the Foundation and their locations, functions, and current status are known to few Foundation personnel outside of the O5 Council."

+ show block
– hide block

Also SCP Foundation: "Full list of all Thaumiel class objects"

item.title: Re: SCP-3999 is Apollyon
item.content:encoded: As is one of Dave's - Js.

item.title: Re: SCP-3999 is Apollyon
item.content:encoded: S.D. Locke's Proposal is Apollyon. Did everyone forget about that? : (

item.title: Re: SCP-3999 is Apollyon
item.content:encoded: Though everything mentioned thus far are good reasons why Apollyon ought not be listed yet, it's also probably worth pointing out that 3999's Object Class likely isn't "legitimate" in an in-universe sense, given the nature of the anomaly/article. IMO, there's only one actual Apollyon (2317) and one meta reference to it (3999).

item.title: Re: SCP-3999 is Apollyon
item.content:encoded:
I guess, "Apollyon" is:

  • Apollyon. Objects that can not be contained, and special containment procedures not applicable to it. Many objects in the Apollyon class can lead to an event of class "K" (the end of the world, doomsday). Terminate or containment objects of this class — primary objective of the SCP Foundation and GOC.

item.title: Re: 2014 Guide Overhaul
item.content:encoded:
In addition to tretter's excellent explanation, you need to remember that authors are free to make shit up as long as it gets accepted by the community, but that doesn't mean it becomes canon (or as canon as stuff can become on this site) in any way. The page you're commenting on lists that canon only.

Basically this site is that one cliche episode in any fantasy or sci-fi series where what the characters think becomes real. ;)

Anyway, if you want the best answer ask the authors. They'll know what they meant.

item.title: Re: 2014 Guide Overhaul
item.content:encoded:
Heimal isn't an official class. It technically falls into the narrative object class category, along with Maksur, Apollyon, and most other SCPs that use one off classes. That means that it's there to give a hint to what's going to happen in the article and set the initial tone.]

In regards to definition, the best one I've heard is that Heimal is the Foundation containing something that was already containing or counteracting another anomaly. While this could technically fall into the SEKT spectrum, I do like seeing more narratively driven classes.

item.title: Re: 2014 Guide Overhaul
item.content:encoded: Hey guys, I have found 2 articles with Hiemal classifications. Is that a secondary class? Is it a tertiary class? What does Hiemal class even mean?

item.title: Re: SCP-3999 is Apollyon
item.content:encoded: True and true.

item.title: Re: SCP-3999 is Apollyon
item.content:encoded:
But advertising Apollyon, so to speak, just increases the potential for the site being flooded by shitty articles.

What's more likely to happen is that a bunch of the shitty Keter articles we'll get will turn into shitty Apollyon articles instead.

Anyway, at this stage, two SCPs is not "enough" in my mind to put Apollyon up there, especially since the idea of what Apollyon means has yet to be established site-wide.

item.title: Re: SCP-3999 is Apollyon
item.content:encoded: I agree. We don't need to hype object classes, if they're good authors will use them.

item.title: Re: SCP-3999 is Apollyon
item.content:encoded: I don't feel like Apollyon should be made mainstream, so to speak. I reckon it kinda goes against the whole purpose of the Foundation to have skips that are completely uncontainable as a widely-accepted class, a la Thaumiel. The amount of bad skips that can't be contained really shows something about that. I feel like Apollyon can only be pulled off successfully in a few instances by good writers, otherwise it's just an excuse to give poor writers a fancier word for "my SPC is very handsome and cant be contained so just let him walk around Site-19." If writers reckon they can pull off Apollyon successfully, I say by all means let them. But advertising Apollyon, so to speak, just increases the potential for the site being flooded by shitty articles.

item.title: SCP-3999 is Apollyon
item.content:encoded: Now that there's two official SCPs with the Apollyon class, could we possibly add it to the official list as an Esoteric Class? Thaumiel looks pretty lonely right there…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: *Removing comment meant for another page*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Euclid section could also mention that it often gets used for skips that are hard to hide without being actively uncooperative. Big Dumb Objects, that sort of thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think we should add a new class for objects that are possible thuamiels, but require more research to be used in such a manner without unforeseen consequences.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Here's a version that's longer and shorter:

Keter-class objects are anomalies that pose a clear existential threat to the Foundation's mission, and by extension, normalcy, human society, and/or the human species. Keter-class anomalies are also difficult or impossible to fully contain using the Foundation's current technology, resources, and knowledge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This guide needs to be rewritten. It was never quite accurate, and didn't undergo the same thorough editing process as the MTF, Sites, and Clearance Levels Guide.

It's basically saying that "Keter is a Danger Rating", despite being described otherwise in the edit notes. (Note: I don't think that was the intent.)

For now, a suggested rephrase:

Keter-class objects are anomalies that pose an severe threat to the existence of humanity or human society, and are difficult or impossible to fully contain using the Foundation's current technology, resources, and knowledge. An anomaly that cannot be reliably contained and could likely have a severe impact on human society is Keter.

…and keep the second paragraph intact.

EDIT: This needs some edits — will come back to this later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After some discussion with staff and a suggestion from thedeadlymoose, we'll go with "inherently serious threat".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Which word is TroyL having an issue with? The word "inherently" isn't a complicated one, so my guess is "inimical"… which raises the question of why it wasn't changed before. If it's the former, however, then there is the synonym "innately".

That said, even I didn't know what "inimical" meant (because I never knew it was a word), and it still gave me the impression of "Bad with a capital B", mostly because I assumed it shared roots (probably Latin) with the word "enmity".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
From TroyL, in chat:

Why use a million dollar word when a five-cent word will work? Try to remember that Guides are made for people with little to no knowledge of the site, from any number of backgrounds.

Why not just "serious threat"?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think I'm fine with that. It helps nail down what it is to be keter while at the same time still granting the flexibility to not pigeonhole writers. Because ultimately we need to both understand why classes need to be codified IC and why they need to be vague OOC.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm good with "inherently inimical threat". Let me get some other staff members to sign off on that, and we'll get the wording fixed up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not saying it shouldn't be a Keter. I'm just saying that its threat to humanity is indirect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The Foundation's mission is to prevent anomalies from ruining humanity's dominance in the world order and existence. SCP-2122 would pose a threat to human existence, thus threatening the Foundation's mission.

If humanity's place is severely affected beyond repair, what's the point of the Foundation then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Some Keters that I've come across aren't direct threats to the Foundation, it's personnel, and/or mission, though. Consider SCP-2112, for instance; its anomalous doesn't directly threaten the survival of the human race, but they do pose an "severe existential threat" to its very culture by reducing all of its media to endless bad-quality reenactments of the same set of songs forever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, the opposite of "empty threat" is "serious threat". Alternatively, I would suggest "inherently inimical threat", because the general idea I got from the scips that I find to be properly classified as Keter (which is fortunately a lot of them) is that they are a threat to the Foundation/humanity/the universe by their very nature (example: SCP-682, perennially hostile to almost anything and everything alive in this universe), as opposed to a Euclid can be considered "mostly safe" despite its unpredictability unless improperly handled, unnecessarily provoked, or otherwise given a particular stimulus that "sets off" their hostile state (example: SCP-1609, which is only hostile if attacked or reminded of its traumatic experience at the hands of the GOC).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Direct threat? Using the likes of 682 and 173 as examples, the former would pose a bigger more direct threat to the Foundation on a larger scale as compared to the latter.

SCPs that are essentially uncontained (usually Keter) also possess direct threats to the Foundation, its personnel and/or mission.
